audienceSections.normal: In Thandi and Sipho introducing themselves, you will use [[grammar:ngi- + ngu- + N]]. Use [[grammar:ngi- + ngu-]] with a name or role to say ‘I am ...’. Ngingumfundi means ‘I am a student.’ The pieces join into one written word, so do not separate ngi and ngu with spaces.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:ngi- + ngu- + N]] in Thandi and Sipho introducing themselves. First-person singular subject concord ngi- combines with identifying copulative ngu- before a vowel-initial noun. The resulting phonological word is written conjunctively.

audienceSections.normal: Use [[grammar:ngi- + ngu-]] with a name or role to say ‘I am ...’. Ngingumfundi means ‘I am a student.’ The pieces join into one written word, so do not separate ngi and ngu with spaces.
audienceSections.expert: First-person singular subject concord ngi- combines with identifying copulative ngu- before a vowel-initial noun. The resulting phonological word is written conjunctively.

audienceSections.normal: Read the joined isiZulu word as a whole, then use the explanation to notice its noun-class or subject-concord parts. Do not remove a concord from a sentence just because the English translation has no matching word.
audienceSections.expert: The analysis treats concord-bearing surface words as morphosyntactic occurrences. Noun citation forms retain their noun-class prefix, and verbs retain the infinitive uku- citation form in lexical records.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact zulu line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:ngi- + ngu- + N]] where it appears.

- Sawubona. — Hello.
- Yebo, sawubona. — Yes, hello.
- NginguThandi. — I am Thandi.
- NginguSipho. — I am Sipho.
- Ngingumfundi, unjani? — I am a student; how are you?
- Nginguthisha, ngiyaphila. — I am a teacher; I am well.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:ngi- + ngu- + N]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.

- Sawubona. — Hello.
- Yebo, sawubona. — Yes, hello.
- NginguThandi. — I am Thandi.
- NginguSipho. — I am Sipho.
- Ngingumfundi, unjani? — I am a student; how are you?
- Nginguthisha, ngiyaphila. — I am a teacher; I am well.
